content.text: React.StrictMode is a development-only tool that intentionally runs parts of your application twice to surface bugs that would otherwise hide until they cause real problems in production. It is not a component in the visual sense — it renders no DOM elements and has zero effect on the production build. Its only output is extra checks and console warnings in the development environment.

attrs.alt: React.StrictMode in development: double-invokes render and effects to catch impurity, logs deprecation warnings for unsafe APIs, zero performance cost in production
attrs.title: What React.StrictMode does

You can also enable it on a sub-tree. For example, if you are migrating a legacy component, wrap only the new code in StrictMode while the old code remains outside:
In development, StrictMode renders every component

. The first render produces the output you see. The second render is discarded — its purpose is to verify that calling the render function twice with the same inputs produces the same result. This enforces referential transparency (purity).

StrictMode simulates an immediate unmount and remount of every component: it runs the effect setup, then the cleanup, then the setup again. This verifies that your cleanup function fully reverses the setup, making effects safe to re-run:
StrictMode warns when your code uses APIs that are deprecated or scheduled for removal in a future React version. These warnings appear in the browser console and do not affect the UI. Common ones:
Why the Double Render Happens (and Why You See It)

React 18 activates concurrent rendering features that can pause, discard, or re-start a render at any time. Components must be pure so React can safely do this. The double-invoke in StrictMode is a deliberate simulation of that behavior during development to surface side-effects in render before they cause subtle bugs under concurrency.
You will notice it most visibly when console logs appear twice:

This is expected and correct. The duplicate log is proof that your render is pure (both renders produced the same visible output). Do not remove StrictMode to silence the duplicate logs — the duplication is the check working as intended.
Common Patterns That Break Under StrictMode
Incrementing an external counter in render
Subscribing to an event bus without unsubscribing
Third-party libraries that are not idempotent

Some older libraries (map widgets, rich-text editors, animation frameworks) initialize themselves by attaching to the DOM and throw errors or behave incorrectly when mounted twice. This is a bug in the library. The options are:
Update the library to a version that supports concurrent React.
Guard initialization with a ref flag:
and return early if already initialised.
As a last resort, temporarily remove StrictMode from that subtree while debugging, but add a comment explaining why, and plan to fix the underlying issue.
The Correct Fix for "Ran Twice" Bugs
When something appears to run twice and that causes a visible problem, the question to ask is:
why does running this twice break my app?
The answer is always that the code is impure or missing a cleanup. The fix is always in the code, never in removing StrictMode.
StrictMode in React 18 vs Earlier Versions
A component's render is pure when:
It only reads from its props, state, and context — no external mutable variables.
It does not mutate existing objects: create new objects or arrays rather than mutating state.
It has no observable side effects: no network calls, no DOM manipulation, no writing to external variables.
Given the same inputs, it always returns the same JSX.
An effect is idempotent when running setup → cleanup → setup leaves the world in the same state as running setup once. Common patterns:

No. StrictMode should remain in production source code. The only case where removing it is justified is as a temporary debugging step — for example, to confirm whether a particular warning comes from StrictMode's double-render or from a real runtime issue. Always restore it after the check.

If StrictMode breaks your app, your app was already broken. StrictMode surfaces the bug; it does not cause it. The fix is to make the component pure and its effects idempotent — not to remove StrictMode.
StrictMode catches runtime behaviors by running code twice; it cannot catch things that need static analysis. Use it alongside, not instead of:
— catches missing dependencies in useEffect, useCallback, useMemo.
— warns about key prop, JSX-in-loops, prop-types.
— prevents passing wrong types to components.

StrictMode's double-invocation behavior surfaces in tests as well. If your test environment also wraps components in StrictMode (React Testing Library does not by default), you may observe similar side-effect doubling.
helper does NOT wrap in StrictMode. You can opt in globally:
Using this wrapper ensures tests catch the same bugs StrictMode would expose in the browser. Any test that passes in strict mode is guaranteed to be pure.
Because effects run setup → cleanup → setup in StrictMode, your tests should also verify that unmounting a component cleans up correctly:

React 18 introduced the concurrent renderer, and StrictMode in React 18 specifically prepares you for concurrent mode's rendering characteristics. Understanding why this matters helps you write code that will not break as React's scheduler evolves.

In concurrent mode React can pause, abort, or restart a render mid-flight without committing the interrupted render to the DOM. If your render function has side effects (mutations, API calls, timer starts), those side effects would fire during the aborted render and leave the application in an inconsistent state.
: React may start rendering a component, then discard it because a higher-priority update arrives. Only pure renders survive this correctly.

: The reconciler can reuse component fibers across render phases. A component might be initialized, paused, and resumed with a different state value — purity ensures the UI reflects the final state, not an intermediate one.
marks non-urgent updates. React can render these in the background while keeping the current UI interactive. If render has side effects, background renders corrupt state before they are committed.

Suspense boundaries interact with StrictMode's double-effect rule. When a component suspends, React unmounts its subtree (running cleanup) and re-mounts it once data is ready (running setup again). This is not StrictMode-specific — it happens in production too. StrictMode's double-effect in development is a deliberate simulation of this exact lifecycle to ensure you handle it correctly.
React's console output in StrictMode can be noisy. Here are reliable strategies for diagnosing each category of warning.
fetch in useEffect with no abort
Add AbortController or use a data-fetching library
Increment in render body or initializer
Move mutation into useEffect or event handler
Return cleanup function that calls removeEventListener
WebSocket created in effect, not closed on cleanup
Migrate to componentDidMount or function component
subscribe() called without unsubscribe() in cleanup
Using the React DevTools profiler to isolate strict-mode renders

The React DevTools Profiler panel marks StrictMode's extra render in a lighter shade. You can record a session and filter to the component in question to see exactly which renders were 'real' (committed) versus 'discarded' (extra). This helps distinguish whether a side effect is happening in render or in an effect.
Click the record button and interact with the page
Stop recording — flame chart shows each committed render
StrictMode's discarded second renders are not shown in the Profiler because they are never committed
at the top of the render body to see the full call stack for each invocation during debugging
Applying StrictMode to Part of the Tree

Because StrictMode is a component, you can apply it to a subtree rather than the entire app. This is useful when integrating a third-party library that triggers false positives under StrictMode, or when migrating an existing codebase incrementally.
Treating partial coverage as a permanent solution is an anti-pattern. Track excluded subtrees as technical debt and work to bring them under StrictMode as the legacy code is migrated.

If you author a React component library, StrictMode compatibility is a first-class quality requirement. Consumers who wrap their apps in StrictMode (the majority using Vite or Next.js defaults) will immediately encounter bugs if your library does not pass the double-render and double-effect tests.
: always include a StrictMode wrapper in your component library's test setup.
: add the Strict Mode decorator to your preview configuration so stories fail-fast when authoring.
: direct DOM access via refs is fine if the ref target is predictable — but
is banned because it cannot work with concurrent rendering.
: document in your changelog when a release fixes a StrictMode incompatibility.
and leave it there — it costs nothing in production.
StrictMode double-renders components and double-invokes effects (React 18+) to expose impurity and missing cleanup.
If your component breaks under StrictMode, the bug was pre-existing — the fix is in your code, not in disabling StrictMode.
Make render functions pure: same inputs → same output, no side effects.
Make effects idempotent: always return a cleanup function that reverses the setup completely.
Use AbortController to cancel fetch requests in cleanup, or adopt TanStack Query / SWR which handles deduplication automatically.
